content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 109v–109r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_018_page_001.jpg, DEU_018_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 154–155

---

1 The Levitical priests — all the tribe of Levi — shall have no portion or inheritance with Israel. The fire offerings of the LORD and his inheritance they shall eat.

2 They shall have no inheritance among their brothers; the LORD is their inheritance, as he promised them.

3 And this shall be the priests' due from the people, from those offering a sacrifice, whether an ox or a sheep: they shall give to the priest the shoulder and the two cheeks and the stomach.

4 The firstfruits of your grain, your new wine, and your oil, and the first fleece of your sheep, you shall give him.

5 For the LORD your God has chosen him out of all your tribes to stand and minister in the name of the LORD — him and his sons, all his days.

6 And if a Levite comes from any of your towns out of all Israel, where he lives — and he may come whenever his soul desires — to the place that the LORD will choose,

7 then he shall minister in the name of the LORD his God, like all his fellow Levites who stand to minister there before the LORD.

8 They shall have equal portions to eat, besides what he receives from the sale of his patrimony.

9 When you come into the land that the LORD your God is giving you, you shall not learn to do according to the abominable practices of those nations.

10 There shall not be found among you anyone who burns his son or his daughter as an offering, or who practices divination, or tells fortunes, or interprets omens, or is a sorcerer,

11 or who casts a spell, or who consults a medium or a spiritist, or who inquires of the dead.

12 For whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD, and because of these abominations the LORD your God is driving them out before you.

13 You shall be blameless before the LORD your God,

14 for these nations, which you are about to dispossess, listen to fortune-tellers and to diviners. But as for you, the LORD your God has not allowed you to do this.

15 The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers — you shall listen to him —

16 just as you desired of the LORD your God at Horeb on the day of the assembly, when you said: "Let me not hear again the voice of the LORD my God or see this great fire any more, lest I die."

17 And the LORD said to me: "They are right in what they have spoken.

18 I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers. And I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I command him.

19 And it shall be that whoever will not listen to my words that he shall speak in my name, I myself will require it of him.

20 But the prophet who presumes to speak a word in my name that I have not commanded him to speak, or who speaks in the name of other gods — that prophet shall die."

21 And if you say in your heart: "How may we know the word that the LORD has not spoken?" —

22 when a prophet speaks in the name of the LORD, if the word does not come to pass or come true, that is a word that the LORD has not spoken; the prophet has spoken it presumptuously. You need not be afraid of him.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — ishei YHWH ve-nachalato (the fire offerings of the LORD and his inheritance):** "The fire offerings of the LORD and his inheritance they shall eat." The phrase is unusual: the Levites have no land-inheritance (*nachala*) among Israel, but the LORD's own fire offerings (*ishei YHWH*) and his inheritance constitute their portion. LXX: *ta karpomata kyriou kai ten merian autou phagontai* — "they shall eat the offerings and his portion."

**Verse 3 — ha-zeroa, ha-lechayayim, ve-ha-qevah (the shoulder, the two cheeks, and the stomach):** These three portions are the priest's specific due (*mishpat ha-kohanim*) from sacrificed animals. The precision of the anatomical terms reflects the formal legal character of the passage. LXX: *ton brachiona kai ta siagona kai ten enystran* — shoulder, jaws, and stomach.

**Verse 6 — be-khol avat nafsho (whenever his soul desires):** "Whenever his soul desires" for *be-khol avat nafsho* (lit. "in all the desire of his soul"). The Levite's freedom to move to the central sanctuary is unrestricted. LXX: *en pase epithymia tes psyches autou* — exact.

**Verse 8 — mekhiraiw al ha-avot (besides what he receives from the sale of his patrimony):** A difficult phrase. "The sale of his patrimony" for *mekhiraiw al ha-avot* (lit. "his sales upon the fathers"). The Levite retains income from property he may have sold. LXX: *plethyn ten apodememenon* — a different reading; the LXX may reflect a different Vorlage or a difficulty in the Hebrew. LOGOS follows the MT.

**Verse 10 — ma'avir beno u-vitto ba-esh (who burns his son or his daughter as an offering):** "Who burns his son or his daughter as an offering" for the child-sacrifice practice. Molech is not named here but is the likely referent (cf. Lev 18:21; 20:2–5). LXX: *os diagon ton huion autou e ten thygatera autou en pyri* — "who passes his son or daughter through fire."

**Verses 10–11 — The Eight Forbidden Practices:**
1. *qosem qesamim* — "practices divination" (general)
2. *me'onen* — "tells fortunes" (cloud-reading or astrology; *Anan*)
3. *menachesh* — "interprets omens" (omen-divination)
4. *mekhashef* — "is a sorcerer" (magical arts)
5. *chover chaver* — "casts a spell" (binding/charm-casting)
6. *sho'el ov* — "consults a medium" (necromantic consultation)
7. *yidde'oni* — "a spiritist" (one who knows — a familiar spirit)
8. *doresh el ha-metim* — "inquires of the dead" (necromancy)

The list is the most comprehensive in the Hebrew Bible. LXX follows the MT closely. LOGOS renders each as a distinct activity preserving the Hebrew specificity.

**Verse 13 — tamim tihyeh 'im YHWH Elohekha (you shall be blameless before the LORD your God):** "Blameless" for *tamim* — the same word used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and required of sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3). Wholeness/integrity before God in contrast to the fragmented, manipulative world of divination. LXX: *hosioi esesesthe enantion kyriou tou theou hymon* — "you shall be holy before the LORD your God."

**Verse 15 — navi ka-mokha (a prophet like me):** The climactic declaration of the chapter. Moses promises a prophetic succession, ultimately pointing to a single eschatological figure. The NT applies this promise to Jesus (Acts 3:22; 7:37). The grammar is future tense and indefinite: *yaqim lekha YHWH Elohekha* — "the LORD your God will raise up for you." LXX: *propheten ek ton adelphon sou os eme anastesi soi kyrios ho theos sou* — exact.

**Verse 18 — ve-natatti devarai be-fiv (I will put my words in his mouth):** "I will put my words in his mouth" — the classic prophetic mediation formula. The prophet is not an independent oracle but a mouthpiece: the divine words are placed in his mouth by YHWH. Compare Jer 1:9. LXX: *doso ta remata mou en to stomati autou* — exact.

**Verse 20 — zadon (presumptuously):** "The prophet who presumes to speak" — the same root (*zadon*) used in 17:12 for presumptuous defiance of the court. The false prophet and the recalcitrant judger are both guilty of *zadon* — willful overstepping of divine authority. The criterion for the true prophet: his words come to pass (*yavo ha-davar*). LXX: *asebese* — "acts impiously."

**Verse 22 — lo tagur mimmenu (you need not be afraid of him):** "You need not be afraid of him" for *lo tagur mimmenu* (lit. "you shall not fear/tremble from him"). The false prophet, whose word fails, loses all authority. This verse closes the chapter with a practical test that grounds prophetic authority in fulfilled speech — but in the larger context of ch. 13, it is subordinate to the criterion of covenant fidelity.

*— LOGOS*
